text: The surgeon uses a Ligasure device to take down adhesions from a previous hysterectomy, revealing adhesions between the sigmoid colon and pelvic structures. The carcinoid mass is visible, showing significant cicatrization of the mesentery and bowel loops.

The video shows the impression of a mesenteric nodal mass at the base of the knot, located at the root of the transverse mesocolon. The surgeon attempts to visualize the path of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).

The surgeon uses a marker tape to measure the bowel, noting the length of ileum distal and proximal to the mass. Over a meter of ileum is visible downstream, with a significant portion entangled in the mass.
The surgeon measures approximately four meters of bowel upstream to the duodenal jejunal flexure, with the video skipping forward to show the flexure.
The surgeon uses Livac slings, which are elasticized bands with hooks, to lay out the bowel around the mass. The slings are adjusted by selecting different holes in the band.

The surgeon catches the peritoneum and the peritoneum of the mesentery, applying a second Livac sling with an adjustable hook end. A third Livac sling is then applied, demonstrating their ease of use and repositioning capability.
The surgeon lays out the mesentery more clearly and begins defining the transverse mesocolon to locate the proximal superior mesenteric artery (SMA).

The surgeon scores the peritoneum over the small bowel mesentery on the patient's left side of the mass, attempting to locate the vessels supplying the involved segment of ileum. The fatty peritoneum presents a challenge in identifying the vessels visually.
The surgeon adjusts the hooks of the Livac slings to reposition the mesentery under tension, facilitating the exposure of the plane behind the transverse mesocolon mesentery.
The surgeon navigates through the fatty tissue and identifies the origin of the right colic vessels, entering the retroperitoneal plane.

The surgeon begins to visualize the vascular anatomy within the mesentery of the small bowel on the patient's left side of the tumor mass. Dissection progresses towards the intended line of transection, primarily within the fatty peritoneal plane of the mesentery.
The surgeon checks the length of the small bowel upstream of the tumor mass, using slings to stabilize the mesentery and prevent devascularization of the proximal small bowel.
A vein becomes visible as the surgeon dissects towards the posterior leaf of the mesentery, creating an opening in the tissue plane. The hook is adjusted to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the main branch of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) supplying the tumor mass, noting its continuation downwards to supply the bowel proximal to the mass. The sizable vessel is visible, indicating the limit of dissection to avoid devascularizing the bowel.

The surgeon uses a Covidien tan cartridge tri-stapler, 30 millimeters, to transect a large vessel visible in the surgical field. The vessel is part of the SMA pathway, extending from the root of the transverse mesocolon towards the bottom of the screen. The dissection is carefully limited to avoid devascularizing the proximal small bowel.

The surgeon continues the mesenteric dissection towards the planned proximal line of bowel transection, confirming that this section of bowel is not supplied by the ileocolic vessels. The hooks and slings used for stabilization are set aside as the layout of the bowels is checked.

The surgeon plans the distal line of transection, with the right colic and ileocolic vessels visible. A combination of clips and ligature is used to dissect the sizable vascular arcades, maintaining a long distance from the tumor mass.

The surgeon divides the proximal end of the planned transection using a tan cartridge Covidian tristapler, followed by the distal transection. The specimen, a large section of bowel, is placed into a Covidian fifteen millimeter ender catch bag. The twelve millimeter port is removed from the gel point gel, and the catch bag is passed through the gel, allowing the specimen to slide out smoothly against the Alexis. The surgeon re-examines the distal margin, considering taking additional margin.

The surgeon observes a reasonable length of terminal ileum leading to the ileocecal valve. A large mesenteric window is visible, which the surgeon closes using a V-lock stitch. The thick and fatty peritoneum of the mesentery is noted, minimizing the risk of needle penetration into significant vessels.
The surgeon completes the mesenteric closure and uses a two-row Prolene suture to align the bowel. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed in the Barcelona style.

The surgeon uses a purple, 60mm tri-stapler cartridge to create two enterotomies, aligning the bowel with the proline stitch to ensure anti-mesenteric borders are matched. The transverse firing is completed with a tan cartridge due to increased vascular supply in the mesentery. A two ovicral crutch stitch is applied at the weak point in the crutch, ensuring it is snug to prevent cutting through the tissue and relieve tension from the staple line.
